did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model based on EfficientNetV2B3 with transfer learning and fine-tuning for skin cancer classification and segmentation. It also employed Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) for visual explanation and k-means clustering for image segmentation. The study achieved accuracies of 84.91% (9 classes), 94.00% (malignant/benign), 89.56% (6 benign classes), and 96.74% (3 malignant classes) using the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing the 'EfficientSkinCaSV2B3' framework for skin cancer classification and segmentation. It details a specific methodology involving transfer learning, fine-tuning of the EfficientNetV2B3 model, Grad-CAM visualization, and k-means clustering. The study reports original results, including accuracy metrics (e.g., 84.91%, 94.00%) on the ISIC dataset, and compares these findings with state-of-the-art methods, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: EfficientSkinCaSV2B3: An Efficient Framework Towards Improving Skin Classification and Segmentation
journal: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications
brief_summary: This study proposes the EfficientSkinCaSV2B3 framework, a custom Convolutional Neural Network based on EfficientNetV2B3, to improve skin cancer classification and segmentation. Utilizing transfer learning and fine-tuning, the model was trained on a dataset of 2,357 images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). The research employed Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) for visual explanation and k-means clustering for segmenting healthy versus unhealthy skin regions. The model achieved accuracies of 84.91% for nine-class classification, 94.00% for malignant versus benign classification, 89.56% for six benign classes, and 96.74% for three malignant classes. These results demonstrate the framework's effectiveness in supporting dermatological diagnosis and treatment planning.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (9 classes, 6 benign classes, 3 malignant classes, and 2 classes: benign vs malignant)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on classifying skin images into discrete diagnostic categories using a custom CNN (EfficientSkinCaSV2B3). It reports accuracy metrics for nine-class classification (84.91%), malignant vs. benign classification (94.00%), six benign classes (89.56%), and three malignant classes (96.74%).

The paper employs k-means clustering for image segmentation to partition images into clusters representing normal surface, abnormal surface, and background based on pixel intensity/color/texture, aiding in the delineation of cancerous areas.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of skin cancer images collected and validated by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). It contains high-resolution images of malignant and benign oncological diseases, curated for the development of automated machine learning and deep learning algorithms.

use.training: Used for training the EfficientSkinCaSV2B3 model via transfer learning and fine-tuning across four classification scenarios (9 classes, 2 classes, 6 benign classes, 3 malignant classes).
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (split 8-1-1).
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance (split 8-1-1).
use.other: Used for k-means clustering segmentation to distinguish healthy vs. unhealthy skin regions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made based on accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Dataset (Nine Classes).description: Classification of Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Vascular Lesion.
ISIC Dataset (Nine Classes).notes: The custom model achieved the highest test accuracy in the fine-tuning phase (84.91%), marking a 19.32% increase over its transfer learning performance.

ISIC Dataset (Two Classes: Benign vs Mal.description: Classification of benign and malignant skin diseases.
ISIC Dataset (Two Classes: Benign vs Mal.notes: The custom model achieved the highest test accuracy in the fine-tuning phase (94.00%), an increase of 7.8% compared to transfer learning.

ISIC Dataset (Six Benign Classes).description: Classification of Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Nevus, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Vascular Lesion.
ISIC Dataset (Six Benign Classes).notes: ResNet50 and EfficientNetB3 showed slightly better performance in the fine-tuning phase (90.40% and 91.58% respectively) compared to the custom model (89.56%).

ISIC Dataset (Three Malignant Classes).description: Classification of Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma.
ISIC Dataset (Three Malignant Classes).notes: The custom model achieved a significant increase in test accuracy of 96.74% in the fine-tuning phase (a growth of 13.03% over transfer learning), outperforming EfficientNetB3 (94.79%) and ResNet50 (93.49%).

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning using pre-trained EfficientNetV2B3 weights

custom_layers: Dense layer with 9 units and softmax activation
architecture_details.description: The model combines the pre-trained EfficientNetV2B3 backbone with additional custom layers including GlobalMaxPooling2D, Batch Normalization, Dropout, and Dense layers with ReLU and softmax activations to adapt the model for skin cancer classification.

training_details.training_schedule: Transfer learning phase followed by fine-tuning phase on the ISIC dataset (2,357 images)

classification_or_output_head.units: Variable based on scenario (e.g., 9 units for 9-class classification)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to desired dimensions to ensure fixed-size inputs for the neural network
Normalization of pixel values to a standard range (scaling between minimum and maximum pixel values)
Data augmentation via flipping (horizontal or vertical)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated by a certain angle (theta) to increase dataset variability.
Images were flipped horizontally or vertically along the respective axes.
Image contrast was adjusted to make features more discernible.

conclusions: The proposed EfficientSkinCaSV2B3 framework, a custom CNN based on EfficientNetV2B3 using transfer learning and fine-tuning, achieved high accuracy in skin cancer classification tasks on the ISIC dataset.

The model achieved a test accuracy of 84.91% for nine-class skin cancer classification, 94.00% for binary classification (benign vs. malignant), 89.56% for six benign classes, and 96.74% for three malignant classes.

In the classification of six benign classes, the EfficientSkinCaSV2B3 model (89.56% accuracy) performed slightly lower than ResNet50 (90.40%) and EfficientNetB3 (91.58%), indicating a limitation that requires future improvement.

The model demonstrated superior performance in classifying three malignant classes (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma) with 96.74% accuracy, outperforming EfficientNetB3 (94.79%) and ResNet50 (93.49%).

Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) was effectively used to provide visual explanations of model decisions, highlighting important regions in skin images to aid in understanding and diagnostic confidence.
K-means clustering was successfully employed for image segmentation to distinguish between healthy and unhealthy skin portions based on color and texture.

The study acknowledges that while the model performs well, there is room for improvement, particularly in the six-class benign classification scenario, and aims for test accuracies larger than 90% in future research.
important_remarks: The study relies on a relatively small dataset of only 2,357 images from the ISIC collection, which may limit the generalizability of the model to broader, more diverse clinical populations.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the internal test split of the ISIC dataset, raising concerns about potential overfitting or dataset-specific bias.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for performance comparisons between models, relying instead on point estimates of accuracy.

In the six-class benign classification scenario, the proposed EfficientSkinCaSV2B3 model (89.56% accuracy) underperformed compared to ResNet50 (90.40%) and EfficientNetB3 (91.58%), indicating a specific limitation in distinguishing benign subtypes.

The segmentation component uses k-means clustering based on color and texture, which is a traditional unsupervised method rather than a deep learning-based segmentation network, potentially limiting its precision compared to state-of-the-art segmentation architectures.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; while an 8-1-1 split is mentioned, the paper does not detail whether augmentation or preprocessing steps were applied before or after the split, which could lead to optimistic performance estimates.

The clinical relevance is supported by the use of Grad-CAM for visual explanation, but the study does not include a clinical evaluation or user study with dermatologists to validate the interpretability or diagnostic utility of these explanations.
